aliases: Fuerzas Armadas Revolucionarias de Colombia – Ejército del Pueblo
description: Far-left Marxist-Leninist guerrilla group involved in the Colombian conflict, founded in 1964 from peasant self-defense groups. Funded by kidnap, ransom, extortion, and drug trafficking. Engaged in terrorism and unconventional warfare. Demobilized in 2017 after a peace agreement, though dissident factions remain active.

country_links.evidence_quote: Sporadic presence in other Latin American countries, predominantly Mexico, Paraguay and Bolivia.
country_links.context: The FARC had a sporadic presence in Bolivia.

country_links.evidence_quote: After 21 April 2001 capture of Brazilian drug lord Luiz Fernando da Costa (a.k.a. Fernandinho Beira-Mar) in Colombia, Colombian and Brazilian authorities accused him of cooperating with FARC–EP through the exchange of weapons for cocaine.
country_links.context: FARC cooperated with a Brazilian drug lord, exchanging weapons for cocaine and providing armed protection.

country_links.evidence_quote: a representative of Sinn Féin who was known to be stationed in Cuba.
country_links.context: The FARC had a connection with an IRA representative stationed in Cuba.

country_links.evidence_quote: On 1 March 2008, Raul Reyes, a member of FARC's ruling Secretariat, was killed just across the border from Colombia, in the small village of Santa Rosa, Ecuador, after Colombian planes bombarded a FARC camp there.
country_links.context: The FARC-EP had a camp in Ecuador that was bombarded by Colombian forces, killing a FARC Secretariat member.

country_links.evidence_quote: they vanished in December 2004 while on bail and returned to Ireland.
country_links.context: The FARC received training from IRA members who were arrested in Colombia and later returned to Ireland.

country_links.evidence_quote: On 11 October 2012 Jamal Yousef, a.k.a. 'Talal Hassan Ghantou', a native of Lebanon, was sentenced to 12 years in prison for conspiring to provide military-grade weapons to the Fuerzas Armadas Revolucionarias de Colombia (the FARC), in exchange for over a ton of cocaine.
country_links.context: A Lebanese national conspired to provide weapons to FARC in exchange for cocaine.

country_links.evidence_quote: The FARC built a deep relationship with the Gaddafi government in Libya, throughout the 1990s and 2000s... The FARC in 2000 requested a $100 million loan from Tripoli, FARC commanders were hosted in Libya...
country_links.context: FARC built a relationship with the Gaddafi government, requested a loan, and had commanders hosted there.

country_links.evidence_quote: Sporadic presence in other Latin American countries, predominantly Mexico, Paraguay and Bolivia.
country_links.context: The FARC had a sporadic presence in Mexico.

country_links.evidence_quote: Incursions into Peru, Venezuela, Brazil, Panama, and Ecuador.
country_links.context: The FARC made incursions into Panama.

country_links.evidence_quote: Sporadic presence in other Latin American countries, predominantly Mexico, Paraguay and Bolivia.
country_links.context: The FARC had a sporadic presence in Paraguay.

country_links.evidence_quote: On 15 July 2018, the Colombian and Peruvian governments launched a joint military effort known as Operation Armageddon to combat FARC dissidents. Peru issued a 60-day state of emergency in the Putumayo Province...
country_links.context: FARC dissidents were targeted by a joint Colombian-Peruvian military operation in Peru.

country_links.evidence_quote: On 22 March 2006 the Attorney General Alberto Gonzales announced the indictment of fifty leaders of FARC for exporting more than $25 billion worth of cocaine to the United States and other countries.
country_links.context: FARC exported cocaine to the United States and was indicted for drug trafficking there.

country_links.evidence_quote: John 40 and their dissident 43rd Front moved into the Amazonas state of western Venezuela. Venezuela has served as the primary location for many FARC dissidents.
country_links.context: FARC dissidents moved into Venezuela, which serves as a primary location for them.
